questions.question:
Out of the following 5 sentences, select the most suitable sentence with respect to grammar and usage:

A. Since the report lacked needed information, it was of no use to them.
B. The report was useless to them because there were no needed information in it.
C. Since the report did not contain the needed information, it was not useful to them.
D. Since the report lacked needed information, it would not have been useful to them.
questions.answer: A. Since the report lacked needed information, it was of no use to them.
questions.to_find: The grammatically and contextually correct sentence about a report lacking information

understand.plain: This question tests your ability to identify the grammatically correct sentence. The key issues to check are subject-verb agreement, correct use of articles and quantifiers, proper verb tense, and logical sentence structure. Option A is clean, concise, and grammatically correct. Options B, C, and D contain subtle errors or awkward phrasing.

keywords.explain: A sentence is grammatically correct when its subject and verb agree, articles/determiners are used properly, and tense is consistent and logical.
keywords.example: Correct: 'There was no information.' Incorrect: 'There were no information.' (information is uncountable)

keywords.explain: 'Information' is an uncountable (mass) noun in English and must be used with singular verb forms and cannot be preceded by 'no needed' in a count sense.
keywords.example: 'There was no useful information' is correct; 'there were no informations' is wrong.

keywords.explain: 'Since' and 'because' both indicate cause, but 'since' is used at the start of a clause that comes before the result, making A a well-structured causal sentence.
keywords.example: 'Since it rained, the match was cancelled.' — the cause clause is placed first.

understand.visual_alt: No figure needed; the question is purely text-based sentence analysis.

given.aim: Select the sentence that is grammatically and idiomatically correct in standard English

terms.meaning: Did not have the required/necessary information
terms.example: The manual lacked needed details, so the technician was confused.
terms.connects: Option A uses this phrase cleanly; it is the standard correct phrasing.

terms.meaning: Grammatically incorrect — 'information' is uncountable and must use 'was' not 'were', and 'no needed' is an awkward collocation
terms.example: Correct form: 'there was no needed information' or 'there was no necessary information'.

terms.meaning: A conditional perfect form suggesting a hypothetical, which is inconsistent with the factual 'lacked' in the same sentence
terms.example: 'The map lacked detail, so the journey would not have been easy.' — mixing simple past fact with conditional perfect is awkward.
terms.connects: This tense inconsistency makes Option D stylistically and logically weak.

given.plan: Check each option for (1) subject-verb agreement with uncountable nouns, (2) tense consistency, (3) correct use of causal connectors, and (4) natural idiomatic phrasing to identify A as correct.

steps.title: Check Option B for grammar
steps.apply: 'There were no needed information' — 'information' is uncountable, so the verb must be 'was', not 'were'. This is a subject-verb agreement error. Option B is eliminated.
steps.note: Uncountable nouns always take singular verbs: 'information was', not 'information were'.

steps.title: Check Option C for redundancy and clarity
steps.apply: Option C says 'did not contain the needed information, it was not useful'. While not outright wrong, the double negative phrasing 'did not contain... not useful' is verbose. Compared to A's direct 'lacked... of no use', C is less precise and clean.
steps.note: Option C is weak compared to A because 'lacked' (Option A) more directly implies absence than 'did not contain'.

steps.title: Check Option D for tense consistency
steps.apply: 'Since the report lacked needed information, it would not have been useful' mixes simple past fact ('lacked') with the conditional perfect ('would not have been'), creating a logical inconsistency. Option D is eliminated.
steps.note: Conditional perfect is used for counterfactuals, not factual past consequences.

steps.title: Confirm Option A as the correct answer
steps.apply: Option A: 'Since the report lacked needed information, it was of no use to them.' — uses 'since' correctly as a causal connector, 'lacked' as the past tense verb, 'needed information' as the proper noun phrase, and 'was of no use' as a grammatically clean predicate. No errors found.
steps.note: All elements — tense, grammar, logic, idiom — are correct in Option A.

solution.result: Option A is the most suitable sentence: 'Since the report lacked needed information, it was of no use to them.'

questions.formula_note: This is a grammar and usage question; no mathematical formulas are applicable.

questions.question:
Out of the following four sentences, select the most suitable sentence with respect to grammar and usage.

A. I will not leave the place until the minister does not meet me.
B. I will not leave the place until the minister doesn't meet me.
C. I will not leave the place until the minister meet me.
D. I will not leave the place until the minister meets me.
questions.answer: D. I will not leave the place until the minister meets me.
questions.to_find: The grammatically correct sentence using 'until' in a negative context

understand.plain: The key issue here is the correct use of 'until' with a negative main clause. When the main clause is already negative ('will not leave'), the subordinate clause after 'until' should be positive — no extra 'not' or 'doesn't' is needed or correct. Additionally, subject-verb agreement requires 'the minister meets' (third person singular present) rather than 'meet'.

keywords.explain: In English, when the main clause contains a negative ('will not'), the subordinate clause after 'until' must be positive. Adding 'not' or 'doesn't' to the 'until' clause creates an unintended double negative that reverses the meaning.
keywords.example: 'I will not stop until it works' — correct; 'I will not stop until it doesn't work' — wrong (implies stopping when it fails).

keywords.explain: With a third person singular subject like 'the minister', the present simple verb requires the -s ending: 'meets', not 'meet'.
keywords.example: 'The minister meets the delegation every week.' — third person singular takes -s.

keywords.explain: Clauses introduced by 'until' describe the time limit up to which the main action continues; they use present simple tense (not future) and should match in polarity with the intended meaning.
keywords.example: 'Wait until the light turns green.' — present simple after 'until', not 'will turn'.

understand.visual_alt: No figure needed; the question is purely text-based sentence analysis.

given.aim: Identify the correct sentence where 'until' is used without a double negative and subject-verb agreement is maintained

terms.meaning: When 'will not' appears in the main clause, the 'until' clause must use the positive form to avoid double-negative meaning
terms.example: 'I will not eat until the food arrives.' — 'arrives' is positive, matching the negative 'will not eat'.
terms.connects: This rule eliminates options A ('does not meet') and B ('doesn't meet').

terms.meaning: English present simple requires adding -s to the base verb for third person singular subjects (he/she/it/the minister)
terms.example: 'She runs', 'The teacher explains', 'The minister meets' — all require -s.
terms.connects: This rule eliminates option C ('meet' instead of 'meets') and confirms option D.

terms.meaning: 'I will not leave' expresses that the speaker stays; the 'until' clause sets the condition under which leaving becomes possible
terms.example: 'I will not leave until conditions change.' — leave becomes possible when conditions change (positive event).
terms.connects: The subordinate clause must describe the positive triggering event, confirming D.

given.plan: Step 1: Eliminate A and B (double negatives with 'until'). Step 2: Eliminate C (wrong verb form 'meet'). Step 3: Confirm D has correct positive clause and proper subject-verb agreement.

steps.title: Eliminate double-negative options A and B
steps.apply: A: 'will not leave ... until the minister does not meet me' — 'does not meet' adds a second negative, creating a double negative. The sentence now wrongly implies: I leave when the minister fails to meet. B: 'will not leave ... until the minister doesn't meet me' — same double-negative error with 'doesn't meet'. Both A and B are eliminated.
steps.note: Rule: 'not ... until + positive' means the action happens when the positive condition is met.

steps.title: Eliminate Option C for subject-verb agreement error
steps.apply: 'until the minister meet me' — 'the minister' is third person singular, requiring 'meets' with the -s ending, not the base form 'meet'. Option C is grammatically incorrect.
steps.note: Base form 'meet' is used only with plural subjects or with modal verbs (e.g., 'could meet').

steps.title: Confirm Option D as correct
steps.apply: D: 'I will not leave the place until the minister meets me.' — (1) Main clause is negative ('will not leave'). (2) 'Until' clause is positive ('meets'). (3) Third person singular subject 'the minister' takes 'meets'. All rules satisfied.
steps.note: Option D conveys the intended meaning: staying until the minister positively meets the speaker.

solution.result: Option D: 'I will not leave the place until the minister meets me.' is the correct sentence.

questions.formula_note: This is a grammar and usage question; no mathematical formulas are applicable.

questions.question:
Consider the following sentences:

i. Arun and Aparna are here.
ii. Arun and Aparna is here.
iii. Arun's families is here.
iv. Arun's family is here.

Which of the above sentences are grammatically CORRECT?

A. (i) and (ii)
B. (i) and (iv)
C. (ii) and (iii)
D. (iii) and (iv)
questions.to_find: Which pair of sentences uses correct subject-verb agreement

understand.plain: This question tests subject-verb agreement in two situations: (1) compound subjects joined by 'and' require a plural verb, and (2) collective/family nouns like 'family' are singular and require a singular verb while 'families' is plural requiring a plural verb. Sentences (i) and (iv) both obey these rules, making option B correct.

keywords.explain: When two subjects are joined by 'and', they form a plural compound subject that requires a plural verb ('are'), not a singular verb ('is').
keywords.example: 'Tom and Jerry are friends.' — plural 'are' because two subjects are joined.

keywords.explain: 'Family' is a singular collective noun that refers to the whole group as one unit; it takes a singular verb ('is'). 'Families' is its plural form and would take 'are'.
keywords.example: 'My family is visiting today.' — singular 'is' for the collective noun 'family'.

keywords.explain: In 'Arun's family', the subject is 'family' (singular), so the correct verb is 'is'. In 'Arun's families', the subject would be 'families' (plural), requiring 'are' — making sentence (iii) wrong.
keywords.example: 'The company's policy is clear.' — possessive doesn't change; 'policy' is singular, so 'is'.

understand.visual_alt: No figure needed; the question is purely text-based grammar analysis.

given.aim: Identify sentences with correct subject-verb agreement for compound subjects and collective nouns

terms.meaning: Two persons joined by 'and' form a plural compound subject requiring 'are' — this is correct
terms.example: Similarly: 'Ram and Sita are here.' (compound subject = plural verb)

terms.meaning: Compound subject joined by 'and' cannot take singular 'is' — this violates subject-verb agreement
terms.example: Incorrect: 'John and Mary is late.' Correct: 'John and Mary are late.'

terms.meaning: 'Family' is a singular collective noun, so it correctly takes the singular verb 'is'
terms.example: 'His family is coming for dinner.' — singular verb for collective noun.

given.plan: Evaluate each sentence: sentence (i) — compound subject needs 'are' (correct); (ii) — compound subject wrongly uses 'is' (incorrect); (iii) — plural 'families' wrongly uses 'is' (incorrect); (iv) — singular 'family' correctly uses 'is' (correct). Correct pair = (i) and (iv) = Option B.

steps.title: Analyse sentences (i) and (ii) — compound subject
steps.apply: Sentence (i): 'Arun and Aparna are here.' — 'Arun and Aparna' is a compound subject (two individuals), requiring plural verb 'are'. Correct. Sentence (ii): 'Arun and Aparna is here.' — same compound subject but with singular 'is'. Incorrect.
steps.note: Two subjects joined by 'and' always take a plural verb.

steps.title: Analyse sentences (iii) and (iv) — possessive + noun
steps.apply: Sentence (iii): 'Arun's families is here.' — 'families' is plural (more than one family), so it requires 'are', not 'is'. Incorrect. Sentence (iv): 'Arun's family is here.' — 'family' is a singular collective noun, correctly matched with singular verb 'is'. Correct.
steps.note: The verb must agree with the headword of the subject phrase, not the possessive modifier.

steps.title: Identify the correct pair
steps.apply: Grammatically correct sentences: (i) and (iv). This corresponds to Option B: (i) and (iv).

solution.result: Option B — sentences (i) and (iv) — are grammatically correct.

questions.formula_note: This is a grammar and usage question; no mathematical formulas are applicable.

questions.question:
Consider the following sentences:

i. After his surgery, Raja hardly could walk.
ii. After his surgery, Raja could barely walk.
iii. After his surgery, Raja barely could walk.
iv. After his surgery, Raja could hardly walk.

Which of the above sentences are grammatically CORRECT?

A. (i) and (ii)
B. (i) and (iii)
C. (ii) and (iii)
D. (ii) and (iv)
questions.to_find: Which sentences correctly place adverbs 'hardly' and 'barely' with the modal verb 'could'

understand.plain: This question tests the correct placement of negative adverbs ('hardly', 'barely') relative to modal verbs ('could'). The grammatical rule is that adverbs modifying the main verb should come after the modal verb, not before it. So 'could hardly walk' and 'could barely walk' are both correct, while 'hardly could walk' and 'barely could walk' are non-standard or incorrect in standard modern English.

keywords.explain: In standard English, adverbs that modify the main verb in a modal verb phrase are placed between the modal (could/should/would) and the main verb. They do not precede the modal.
keywords.example: 'She could barely speak.' — 'barely' comes after 'could', before 'speak'. Not 'she barely could speak'.

keywords.explain: 'Hardly' and 'barely' are negative adverbs meaning 'almost not'. They are grammatically similar and follow the same placement rules in modal constructions.
keywords.example: 'He could hardly see in the dark.' and 'He could barely see in the dark.' — both correct.

keywords.explain: The word order Subject + modal + adverb + main verb is standard in positive declarative sentences. Placing the adverb before the modal creates an inverted or emphatic order that is non-standard in plain statements.
keywords.example: 'Raja could hardly walk' is standard; 'Raja hardly could walk' is non-standard/incorrect.

understand.visual_alt: No figure needed; the question is purely text-based adverb placement analysis.

given.aim: Identify which sentences correctly position the adverbs 'hardly' and 'barely' after the modal verb 'could'

terms.meaning: Standard placement: modal 'could' + adverb 'barely' + main verb 'walk'. Grammatically correct.
terms.example: 'After the accident, she could barely move.'

terms.meaning: Standard placement: modal 'could' + adverb 'hardly' + main verb 'walk'. Grammatically correct.
terms.example: 'After the fever, he could hardly stand.'

terms.term: hardly could walk / barely could walk
terms.meaning: Non-standard placement: adverb placed before modal. This is ungrammatical in plain declarative statements.
terms.example: Incorrect: 'She hardly could breathe.' Correct: 'She could hardly breathe.'
terms.connects: Sentences (i) 'hardly could walk' and (iii) 'barely could walk' are incorrect.

given.plan: Check adverb position relative to modal verb 'could' in each sentence. Sentences with adverb after 'could' are correct; those with adverb before 'could' are wrong. Correct: (ii) and (iv). Answer: D.

steps.title: Evaluate sentences (i) and (iii) — adverb before modal
steps.apply: Sentence (i): 'Raja hardly could walk' — 'hardly' is placed before the modal 'could'. This is non-standard. Sentence (iii): 'Raja barely could walk' — 'barely' is placed before 'could'. Same error. Both (i) and (iii) are incorrect.
steps.note: Negative adverbs in modal constructions belong after the modal, not before it.

steps.title: Evaluate sentences (ii) and (iv) — adverb after modal
steps.apply: Sentence (ii): 'Raja could barely walk' — 'barely' comes after 'could'. Standard and correct. Sentence (iv): 'Raja could hardly walk' — 'hardly' comes after 'could'. Standard and correct. Both (ii) and (iv) are correct.
steps.note: Subject + modal + adverb + main verb is the correct word order for standard declarative statements.

steps.title: Select the correct option
steps.apply: Correct sentences are (ii) and (iv) => Option D.
steps.note: Both 'could barely' and 'could hardly' express the same near-impossibility of performing the action.

solution.result: Option D — sentences (ii) and (iv) — are grammatically correct.

questions.formula_note: This is a grammar and usage question; no mathematical formulas are applicable.

questions.question:
Consider the following sentences:

i. The number of candidates who appear for the GATE examination is staggering.
ii. A number of candidates from my class are appearing for the GATE examination.
iii. The number of candidates who appear for the GATE examination are staggering.
iv. A number of candidates from my class is appearing for the GATE examination.

Which of the above sentences are grammatically CORRECT?

A. (i) and (ii)
B. (i) and (iii)
C. (ii) and (iii)
D. (ii) and (iv)
questions.to_find: Which sentences correctly apply 'the number of' vs 'a number of' subject-verb agreement rules

understand.plain: This question tests a classic English grammar rule: 'The number of X' is a singular noun phrase and takes a singular verb (is/was), while 'A number of X' is equivalent to 'many X' and takes a plural verb (are/were). Sentence (i) uses 'the number' with 'is' (correct) and sentence (ii) uses 'a number' with 'are' (correct). Sentences (iii) and (iv) swap the verbs, making them wrong.

keywords.explain: 'The number of X' refers to a specific count as a single quantity. 'The number' is the subject and is singular, so the verb must be singular (is, was, has).
keywords.example: 'The number of errors in the code is surprisingly high.' — singular 'is'.

keywords.explain: 'A number of X' means 'several X' or 'many X'. The head noun is effectively X (plural), so the verb must be plural (are, were, have).
keywords.example: 'A number of students are absent today.' — plural 'are'.

keywords.explain: Quantifier phrases can be tricky: the key is identifying whether the grammatical subject is the quantifier itself (the number = singular) or the noun it quantifies (a number of candidates = candidates = plural).
keywords.example: 'The majority is against the decision.' vs 'A majority of voters are undecided.'

understand.visual_alt: No figure needed; the question is purely text-based grammar analysis.

given.aim: Apply 'the number of' (singular) and 'a number of' (plural) subject-verb agreement rules to identify correct sentences

terms.meaning: 'The number' acts as a singular subject regardless of what follows, requiring a singular verb
terms.example: 'The number of books on that shelf is thirty.'
terms.connects: Sentence (i) uses 'the number ... is staggering' — correct.

terms.meaning: 'A number of' functions like 'many' or 'several', making the following noun the effective plural subject
terms.example: 'A number of issues have been raised during the meeting.'
terms.connects: Sentence (ii) uses 'a number of candidates ... are appearing' — correct.

terms.meaning: Using plural 'are' with 'the number of' is a grammar error; 'the number' demands a singular verb
terms.example: Wrong: 'The number of players are increasing.' Correct: 'The number of players is increasing.'
terms.connects: Sentence (iii) uses 'the number ... are staggering' — incorrect.

given.plan: Step 1: Identify the subject phrase (the number vs a number of). Step 2: Apply singular verb to 'the number of' and plural verb to 'a number of'. Step 3: (i) = correct, (ii) = correct, (iii) = wrong, (iv) = wrong. Answer = Option A.

steps.title: Apply the rule for 'the number of'
steps.apply: Sentence (i): 'The number of candidates who appear ... is staggering.' — Subject is 'the number' (singular) => verb 'is' is correct. Sentence (iii): 'The number of candidates who appear ... are staggering.' — Same subject 'the number' (singular) but verb 'are' (plural) is wrong. (i) correct, (iii) wrong.
steps.note: 'The number' = singular regardless of the noun phrase that follows.

steps.title: Apply the rule for 'a number of'
steps.apply: Sentence (ii): 'A number of candidates from my class are appearing ...' — 'A number of candidates' = 'many candidates' => plural verb 'are' is correct. Sentence (iv): 'A number of candidates from my class is appearing ...' — 'a number of candidates' requires plural 'are', not singular 'is'. (ii) correct, (iv) wrong.
steps.note: 'A number of' = plural, equivalent to 'many' or 'several'.

steps.title: Identify the correct option
steps.note: This is one of the most commonly tested grammar rules in GATE GA: 'the number' is singular, 'a number of' is plural.

solution.result: Option A — sentences (i) and (ii) — are grammatically correct.

questions.formula_note: This is a grammar and usage question; no mathematical formulas are applicable.
